content.problemStatement:
You are given an array AAA of length KKK. Find any permutation PPP of length NNN such that only the prefixes of length AiA_iAi​ (1≤i≤K1 \le i \le K1≤i≤K) form a permutation.

Under the given constraints, it is guaranteed that there exists at least one permutation which satisfies the given condition.

If there are multiple solutions, you may print any.

Note: A permutation of length NNN is an array where every element from 111 to NNN occurs exactly once.


Input Format

The first line of the input contains a single integer TTT - the number of test cases.
The first line of each test case contains two integers NNN and KKK - the length of the permutation to be constructed and the size of the array AAA respectively.
The second line of each test case contains KKK space-separated integers A1,A2,…,AKA_1, A_2, \dots, A_KA1​,A2​,…,AK​ denoting the array AAA.


Output Format
For each test case, print a single line containing NNN space-separated integers P1,…,PNP_1, \dots, P_{N}P1​,…,PN​ (1≤Pi≤N)(1 \le P_i \le N)(1≤Pi​≤N). If there are multiple solutions, you may print any.
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each test case consists of two lines. The first line contains two integers N and K. The second line contains K space‑separated integers A1, A2, …, AK (strictly increasing and AK = N).
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line with N space‑separated integers – a permutation P of length N that satisfies the condition described in the statement.

sampleTestCases.explanation: Test case 1: The constructed permutation is 3 1 2 6 4 5 7 8. Prefixes of lengths 3,6,7,8 contain exactly the numbers 1..L, all other prefixes miss a number because they contain the element 3 or 6 which is larger than the prefix length.\nTest case 2: Only the full length 7 must be a permutation, so we start with 7 followed by the remaining numbers in increasing order.\nTest case 3: Every length is required, therefore the identity permutation works.

solution.approach: We build the permutation segment by segment. Let prev = 0. For each required prefix length a in the array A we consider the numbers (prev+1 … a). To make the prefix of length a a permutation we must place exactly these numbers somewhere in the first a positions. To guarantee that no intermediate prefix (prev < L < a) is a permutation, we put the largest number a first, followed by the remaining numbers in increasing order. After processing all a, the whole array is a permutation of 1…N and only the prefixes whose lengths are listed in A are permutations. This construction always works and runs in O(N) per test case.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N) per test case, O(∑N) over all test cases.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N) for the output array per test case.

code.Python3:
#!/usr/bin/env python3
import sys
def solve():
    data = list(map(int, sys.stdin.read().split()))
    it = iter(data)
    t = next(it)
    out_lines = []
    for _ in range(t):
        n = next(it); k = next(it)
        A = [next(it) for __ in range(k)]
        prev = 0
        ans = []
        for a in A:
            ans.append(a)               # largest first
            for x in range(prev + 1, a):
                ans.append(x)
            prev = a
        out_lines.append(' '.join(map(str, ans)))
    sys.stdout.write('\n'.join(out_lines))
if __name__ == "__main__":
    solve()
